enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted web app that automates searching for and downloading TV shows and movies via Jackett and Transmission.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: nefarious is an open-source, self-hosted web application for discovering and automatically downloading TV shows and movies. It acts as a central hub that connects to Jackett for searching torrent indexers and to Transmission for managing the resulting downloads, giving users a single dashboard instead of juggling separate tools.

The project is maintained on GitHub by developer lardbit under the GPL-3.0 license and is typically deployed with Docker and Docker Compose, storing configuration and library data in persistent volumes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: nefarious suits home server operators who already run Jackett and Transmission and want a friendlier, browser-based front end for finding and queuing content, including households that want multiple users with their own permission groups.

features.title: Content discovery
features.description: Search and browse TV shows and movies by genre, year, and popularity from within the app.

features.title: Quality profiles
features.description: Set preferred resolutions and formats, such as 1080p for movies or 720p for TV, per content type.

features.title: Filtering and blacklisting
features.description: Filter results with keyword rules and blacklist unwanted releases or sources to keep the queue clean.

features.title: Multi-user permissions
features.description: Support for multiple accounts with permission groups so household members get scoped access.

features.title: Subtitle handling
features.description: Automatic subtitle discovery alongside downloaded media.

features.title: Apprise notifications
features.description: Send status and completion alerts through the Apprise notification library, which covers many notification services.

features.title: VPN support
features.description: Route download traffic through a VPN for network privacy.

features.title: Library import
features.description: Import an existing media library so previously downloaded content is recognized.

pros: Free and fully open source under GPL-3.0
Single interface for browsing, quality rules, and downloads instead of managing Jackett and Transmission separately
Multi-user permission groups for shared households
Wide notification coverage via Apprise
cons: Requires Jackett and Transmission to already be configured, which adds setup complexity
Docker/Docker Compose knowledge is effectively required to install
Smaller community and update cadence than the more established *arr ecosystem tools

faqs.question: Is nefarious free?
faqs.answer: Yes. It is open-source software released under the GPL-3.0 license with no purchase or subscription required.

faqs.question: Do I need other software to run nefarious?
faqs.answer: Yes. It depends on Jackett for indexer searches and Transmission for handling downloads; both must be set up alongside it.

faqs.question: How is nefarious installed?
faqs.answer: It is designed to run via Docker and Docker Compose, with a documented environment template for configuration.

faqs.question: Does nefarious support multiple users?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports multiple user accounts with configurable permission groups.

use_cases.title: Automated TV and movie acquisition
use_cases.description: Search once and let nefarious apply quality rules to find and queue matching releases automatically.

use_cases.title: Shared household media requests
use_cases.description: Give family members their own scoped accounts to search and queue content within set permissions.
use_cases.audience: Households running a shared media server

enrichment.seo_meta_description: nefarious is a free, open-source self-hosted web app that automates TV and movie downloads through Jackett and Transmission.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted media request system for Plex and Emby that lets users request movies and TV shows for automatic fulfillment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Ombi is an open-source media request platform created by developer Jamie Rees for Plex and Emby server owners. Users log in with their Plex or Emby credentials, browse a catalog of movies and shows, and submit requests. Ombi checks whether the content already exists and, if not, hands the request off to connected automation tools such as Sonarr and Radarr to fetch it.
The project accepts community contributions on GitHub and is supported through optional donations via PayPal and Patreon, with active support channels on Discord and GitHub issues.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Ombi is built for people running a shared Plex or Emby server for family or friends who don't want to grant everyone direct access to Sonarr, Radarr, or the file system — instead, users simply request titles and get notified when they're ready.

features.title: Plex/Emby user sync
features.description: Import existing users from a connected Plex or Emby server so they can log in with their normal credentials.

features.title: Smart search
features.description: Search for movies and shows across metadata providers, showing availability before a request is submitted.

features.title: Automation integration
features.description: Connects to Sonarr, Radarr, and CouchPotato so approved requests are fetched automatically.

features.title: Request approval workflow
features.description: Optional admin approval step before a request is sent on for fulfillment.

features.title: Notifications
features.description: Configurable alerts to inform requesters and admins about request status changes.

features.title: Mobile apps
features.description: Native iOS and Android apps for browsing and requesting content on the go.

pros: Free and open source with active GitHub and Discord communities
Tight integration with the Sonarr/Radarr automation stack
Native mobile apps in addition to the web interface
Syncs existing Plex/Emby user accounts, avoiding duplicate logins
cons: Requires Sonarr/Radarr (or similar tools) already configured to actually fulfill requests
Self-hosting and reverse-proxy setup is needed for secure remote access
Development pace can vary since it's a community-maintained project

faqs.question: Is Ombi free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ombi is free and open source. The project accepts optional donations but charges no license or subscription fee.

faqs.question: Does Ombi download the media itself?
faqs.answer: No. Ombi manages requests and hands them off to connected tools like Sonarr and Radarr, which handle the actual downloading.

faqs.question: Can users log in with their Plex account?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ombi can sync and authenticate users directly from a connected Plex or Emby server.

faqs.question: Is there a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ombi has native apps for iOS and Android in addition to its web interface.

use_cases.title: Family media requests
use_cases.description: Let household members request new movies and shows without giving them access to the underlying automation tools.

use_cases.title: Moderated content additions
use_cases.description: Use the approval workflow so an admin reviews requests before content is fetched.
use_cases.audience: Server owners managing storage and licensing limits

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ombi is a free, open-source request system that lets Plex and Emby users request movies and shows for automatic fulfillment.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted podcast manager written in Rust that downloads episodes automatically and streams them through a web player.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: PodFetch is an open-source, self-hosted podcast downloader and server built with a Rust backend and a React frontend. It subscribes to podcast feeds, automatically downloads new episodes, and provides a web app for browsing and listening to your archive.

The project is developed by SamTV12345 on GitHub and ships a Docker image that is rebuilt on every push to the main branch, so operators typically pair it with an updater like Watchtower to stay current.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: PodFetch fits self-hosters who want to archive podcasts locally instead of relying on a third-party app, and who want to keep using their preferred podcast client through GPodder-compatible sync rather than being locked into PodFetch's own player.

features.title: Automatic episode downloads
features.description: Subscribes to podcast RSS feeds and pulls new episodes as they're published.

features.title: Web-based player
features.description: Browse and listen to downloaded episodes directly from the PodFetch web interface.

features.title: GPodder integration
features.description: Sync with GPodder-compatible apps so you can keep using an existing mobile podcast client against your self-hosted server.

features.title: Docker-first deployment
features.description: Ships as a continuously updated Docker image for easy self-hosting.

pros: Free and open source
Lightweight Rust backend
GPodder compatibility means no need to switch podcast apps on mobile
Frequent Docker image rebuilds keep it current with the latest commits
cons: Smaller community than mainstream podcast apps, so documentation is thin in places
No official native mobile app — mobile access relies on GPodder-compatible clients
Self-hosting and storage management is on the operator

faqs.question: Is PodFetch free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is open-source software that can be self-hosted at no cost.

faqs.question: Can I use my existing podcast app with PodFetch?
faqs.answer: Yes, PodFetch supports GPodder-compatible sync so existing podcast apps can connect to it.

faqs.question: How is PodFetch deployed?
faqs.answer: It is distributed primarily as a Docker image, rebuilt automatically with each update to the main branch.

use_cases.title: Personal podcast archive
use_cases.description: Automatically download and retain episodes from favorite shows on your own storage.

use_cases.title: GPodder-synced listening
use_cases.description: Keep episode progress and subscriptions synced to a GPodder-compatible mobile app backed by a self-hosted server.
use_cases.audience: Users who want to avoid third-party podcast services

enrichment.seo_meta_description: PodFetch is a free, open-source self-hosted podcast downloader with a web player and GPodder sync support.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source movie collection manager that automatically monitors, searches for, and downloads movies to a quality standard you set.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Radarr is a free, open-source movie collection manager, originally forked from Sonarr's codebase in 2017 by a developer frustrated with CouchPotato. It monitors your movie library, searches configured indexers for matching releases, and sends the best match to a download client, then renames and organizes the file into your library.

Radarr is community-developed and distributed for Windows, Linux (including ARM), macOS, NAS platforms like Synology and QNAP, Docker, and BSD, with an active wiki and forum maintained by the Servarr project.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Radarr is aimed at home media server operators who want their movie library to stay complete and consistently formatted without manually searching for each release, typically alongside a Plex, Jellyfin, or Emby server and tools like Ombi for requests.

features.title: Calendar view
features.description: See upcoming movie releases relevant to your monitored list in one place.

features.title: Manual and automatic search
features.description: Let Radarr search automatically on a schedule, or manually pick a specific release to grab.

features.title: Failed download handling
features.description: Automatically blacklists releases that fail and retries with the next best match.

features.title: Custom formats
features.description: Fine-tune release selection with preferred words, scoring, and regex-based rules.

features.title: Quality profiles
features.description: Define acceptable resolutions and formats per movie or globally.

features.title: Notifications
features.description: Send alerts on grabs, imports, and failures to a wide range of notification services.

features.title: Multiple library views
features.description: Switch between poster grid and table layouts, plus collection-based tracking.

pros: Completely free and open source
Broad platform support including NAS, Docker, and BSD
Large, mature community with extensive wiki documentation
Deep integration with the wider *arr ecosystem (Sonarr, Prowlarr, Bazarr, Ombi)
cons: Requires a separate download client and indexers to be functional
Configuration (quality profiles, custom formats) has a learning curve for new users
No official support channel beyond community forums and Discord

faqs.question: Is Radarr free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Radarr is completely free, open-source software with no paid tier.

faqs.question: Does Radarr download movies itself?
faqs.answer: No, it searches indexers and sends releases to a separate download client (such as a torrent or Usenet client) that you configure.

faqs.question: What platforms does Radarr run on?
faqs.answer: Windows, Linux (including ARM/ARM64), macOS, Docker, BSD, and NAS devices like Synology and QNAP.

faqs.question: Does Radarr work with Plex or Jellyfin?
faqs.answer: Yes, it doesn't stream media itself but organizes files so any media server, including Plex, Jellyfin, and Emby, can read them.

use_cases.title: Automated movie library management
use_cases.description: Monitor a wishlist of movies and have Radarr fetch and organize them as they become available.

use_cases.title: Quality-consistent collections
use_cases.description: Enforce custom formats and quality profiles so an entire movie library matches the same resolution and encoding standards.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Radarr is a free, open-source tool that automatically monitors, searches for, and organizes movies for your media library.

enrichment.short_description: Cross-platform, open-source tool that downloads and copies media from any Plex server you have access to onto your own.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Reaparr is an open-source, GPL-3.0-licensed media downloader that connects to Plex servers you have login access to and copies movies and TV episodes onto your own storage. It can browse libraries in a poster grid or table view, filter by genre, year, quality, and country, and download multiple items in parallel across resumable, resilient queues.

It also works as an indexer and download client for Sonarr and Radarr, letting those tools pull content sourced from other Plex libraries. Reaparr runs via Docker on Windows, Linux, macOS, Unraid, and Synology.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Reaparr is for people with legitimate access to multiple Plex servers — such as friends' or family members' shares — who want to consolidate content locally, or who want an extra source feeding their existing Sonarr/Radarr automation.

features.title: Multi-account, multi-server support
features.description: Connect several Plex accounts and servers at once to pull from more than one library.

features.title: Parallel downloads
features.description: Process multiple queued downloads simultaneously with automatic recovery from interruptions.

features.title: Library browsing and filtering
features.description: Filter and sort available content by genre, year, quality, and country before downloading.

features.title: Sonarr/Radarr integration
features.description: Acts as an indexer and download client so Sonarr and Radarr can fetch content sourced from connected Plex servers.

features.title: Setup wizard
features.description: Guided initial configuration to connect accounts and set download paths.

features.title: Flexible file organization
features.description: Choose where downloaded files are stored on your own system.

pros: Free and open source under GPL-3.0
Consolidates content from multiple Plex servers you already have access to
Plugs directly into an existing Sonarr/Radarr setup
Runs on NAS platforms (Unraid, Synology) as well as desktop OSes via Docker
cons: Only useful if you already have login access to other Plex servers
Docker knowledge required for setup
Raises content-ownership and permission considerations when pulling from servers you don't own

faqs.question: Is Reaparr free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free, open-source software with an optional donation link.

faqs.question: Do I need my own Plex server to use Reaparr?
faqs.answer: You need access (an account) to at least one Plex server whose content you want to copy; Reaparr then organizes downloads into your own storage.

faqs.question: Can Reaparr work with Sonarr or Radarr?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can be configured as an indexer and download client so Sonarr and Radarr can source content through it.

faqs.question: What platforms does Reaparr support?
faqs.answer: It runs via Docker on Windows, Linux, macOS, Unraid, and Synology.

use_cases.title: Consolidating shared Plex libraries
use_cases.description: Pull copies of content from friends' or family members' Plex servers into your own library.
use_cases.audience: Users with access to multiple Plex servers

use_cases.title: Extra source for *arr automation
use_cases.description: Feed Sonarr/Radarr an additional content source by connecting Reaparr as an indexer/download client.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Reaparr is a free, open-source tool that downloads media from any Plex server you have access to and organizes it locally.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source PVR that automatically monitors, searches, downloads, and organizes TV series from Usenet and torrent sources.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Sonarr is a free, open-source internet PVR for TV series. It was renamed from NzbDrone in October 2014 and has since become one of the most widely used tools in the self-hosted media automation ecosystem. Sonarr monitors your show list, watches for new episodes, searches configured indexers, and hands matching releases to a download client before renaming and filing the episode into your library.
Development is community-driven and hosted on GitHub, with official builds for Windows, Linux, macOS, Docker, and NAS devices, plus an extensive wiki maintained by the Servarr project.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Sonarr is built for home media server operators who track ongoing TV series and want new episodes fetched and organized automatically as they air, typically paired with Plex, Jellyfin, or Emby and complementary tools like Radarr, Prowlarr, and Bazarr.

features.title: Episode monitoring
features.description: Track ongoing and future episodes for shows in your library and grab them automatically as they're released.

features.title: Quality profiles
features.description: Define acceptable resolutions and release formats globally or per series.

features.title: Calendar view
features.description: See upcoming episode air dates across all monitored series.

features.title: Manual search
features.description: Manually browse and select specific releases when you want more control than automatic search.

features.title: Failed download handling
features.description: Automatically blacklists problematic releases and retries with the next best candidate.

features.title: Notification system
features.description: Send alerts for grabs, imports, and failures to many third-party notification services.

pros: Completely free and open source
Mature project with a decade of active development and a large community
Broad platform coverage including Docker and NAS devices
Integrates cleanly with the rest of the *arr ecosystem
cons: Requires a separate download client and configured indexers to function
Initial setup (quality profiles, root folders, permissions) has a learning curve
Community-supported only, no official commercial support

faqs.question: Is Sonarr free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sonarr is free, open-source software.

faqs.question: Does Sonarr download episodes directly?
faqs.answer: No, Sonarr searches indexers and sends chosen releases to a separate Usenet or torrent download client that you configure.

faqs.question: What platforms does Sonarr support?
faqs.answer: Windows, Linux (including ARM and musl-based distros), macOS, Docker, and NAS systems such as Synology and QNAP.

faqs.question: Was Sonarr previously called something else?
faqs.answer: Yes, it was known as NzbDrone before being renamed to Sonarr in October 2014.

use_cases.title: Automated TV series tracking
use_cases.description: Monitor ongoing shows and automatically fetch new episodes as they're released.

use_cases.title: Library standardization
use_cases.description: Apply consistent quality profiles and naming across a growing TV library.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Sonarr is a free, open-source PVR that automatically finds, downloads, and organizes TV series for your media library.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted Spotify release tracker that auto-syncs new songs from followed artists into a playlist.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: TrackWatch is an open-source, self-hosted service that connects to your Spotify account, watches the artists you follow, and automatically adds their new releases to a dedicated playlist. It checks for new music multiple times a day so you don't have to manually search for releases.
The project is developed by emlopezr and is available on GitHub, with a hosted demo instance and a Docker image published to both GHCR and Docker Hub for self-hosting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: TrackWatch is for Spotify users who want a private, self-hosted alternative to relying purely on Spotify's own release radar, and who want extra utilities like a discography generator and a tool for cleaning up ghost tracks from playlists.

features.title: Automatic playlist syncing
features.description: New releases from followed artists are added to a TrackWatch-managed playlist automatically.

features.title: Frequent release checks
features.description: Polls for new music multiple times per day rather than a single daily check.

features.title: Discography generator
features.description: Build a playlist covering an artist's full discography.

features.title: Ghost track cleaner
features.description: Detect and remove tracks that have been removed or are no longer playable from playlists.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Runs via Docker, keeping your listening data off third-party servers beyond Spotify itself.

pros: Free and open source
Simple Docker deployment with published images on GHCR and Docker Hub
Checks for releases more frequently than relying on Spotify's own notifications
Extra utilities (discography generator, ghost track cleaner) beyond basic tracking
cons: Only works with Spotify, no support for other streaming services
Requires a Spotify developer app/API credentials to connect your account
Smaller, single-maintainer project with limited documentation

faqs.question: Is TrackWatch free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free, open-source software that you self-host.

faqs.question: Does TrackWatch work with services other than Spotify?
faqs.answer: No, it is built specifically around the Spotify API and playlists.

faqs.question: How do I deploy TrackWatch?
faqs.answer: It's distributed as a Docker image published to GHCR and Docker Hub, deployable in minutes.

faqs.question: What is the ghost track cleaner?
faqs.answer: A utility that finds and removes tracks in your playlists that Spotify has delisted or made unplayable.

use_cases.title: Automatic new-release playlist
use_cases.description: Keep a playlist automatically updated with new songs from artists you follow on Spotify.

use_cases.title: Playlist maintenance
use_cases.description: Clean up broken or delisted tracks and generate full-discography playlists for an artist.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: TrackWatch is a free, open-source self-hosted tool that auto-syncs new Spotify releases from your followed artists into a playlist.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source dashboard for downloading online videos directly to a NAS, with direct mobile-to-NAS sharing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: youtube-dl-nas is an open-source tool that gives a network-attached storage device a web dashboard for downloading online videos, plus a mobile-sharing setup so links and credentials can be shared directly between a phone and the NAS without routing through an external server.
The project is maintained by hyeonsangjeon on GitHub, distributed as Docker images on Docker Hub and GitHub Container Registry, and documented in both English and Korean.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: It's aimed at NAS owners who want to queue video downloads from a phone or browser and have them land directly on their own storage, without sending links through a third-party cloud service.

features.title: Web dashboard
features.description: Manage and monitor video download jobs on the NAS from a browser.

features.title: Mobile sharing setup
features.description: Share links and credentials directly from a phone to the NAS to trigger downloads remotely.

features.title: No intermediary server
features.description: Shared URLs and credentials go directly between the phone and the NAS rather than through an external relay.

features.title: Docker distribution
features.description: Available as images on both Docker Hub and GitHub Container Registry for straightforward self-hosting.

pros: Free and open source
Keeps downloads and shared links private by avoiding a third-party relay
Documented in both English and Korean
Simple Docker-based installation
cons: Niche, single-maintainer project with limited broader community support
Primarily suited to NAS setups rather than general desktop use
Depends on the underlying video-downloading engine staying compatible with source sites

faqs.question: Is youtube-dl-nas free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free, open-source software.

faqs.question: Does it require cloud services to share downloads to my phone?
faqs.answer: No, mobile sharing is designed to work directly between your phone and your NAS without an intermediary server.

faqs.question: How is it installed?
faqs.answer: Via Docker, using images published to Docker Hub or GitHub Container Registry.

use_cases.audience: NAS owners and home server operators

use_cases.title: Mobile-triggered downloads
use_cases.description: Send a video link from a phone directly to the NAS to start a download without cloud intermediaries.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: youtube-dl-nas is a free, open-source dashboard for downloading videos straight to a NAS with direct mobile-to-NAS sharing.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, plugin-based command-line tool that organizes music libraries and enriches metadata via MusicBrainz.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Beets is a free, open-source Python application for managing and cataloging music collections, self-described as being made for "obsessive music geeks." Its core job is to import music, match it against the MusicBrainz database, and correct and enrich tags such as artist, album, genre, and album art, then rename and organize files into a consistent structure.

It's installed via pip and extended through a large plugin ecosystem covering things like lyrics fetching, acoustic fingerprinting, transcoding, duplicate detection, and a web-based library browser with HTML5 playback.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Beets suits music collectors who care about accurate, consistent metadata across large libraries and are comfortable with a command-line tool, including those who pair it with Plex, Jellyfin, or Navidrome as the metadata and organization layer feeding the library.

features.title: MusicBrainz metadata matching
features.description: Automatically identifies tracks and albums against the MusicBrainz database and applies corrected tags.

features.title: Plugin architecture
features.description: Extend functionality with plugins for album art, lyrics, genres, tempo, acoustic fingerprints, and more.

features.title: Multiple metadata sources
features.description: Pull additional metadata from sources like Discogs and Beatport alongside MusicBrainz.

features.title: Duplicate and gap detection
features.description: Identify duplicate tracks and missing entries in albums.

features.title: Transcoding
features.description: Convert audio files between formats through plugin support.

features.title: Web interface
features.description: Optional web-based library browser with HTML5 audio playback.

pros: Free and open source with a mature, active plugin ecosystem
Highly accurate metadata correction via MusicBrainz matching
Cross-platform since it runs anywhere Python does
Extensive documentation and an active community (mailing list, IRC, blog)
cons: Command-line first, which is a barrier for users wanting a pure GUI experience
Initial tagging pass on a large, messy library can take real time and manual review
Plugin quality and maintenance varies since many are community-contributed

faqs.question: Is Beets free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free, open-source software installable via pip.

faqs.question: Does Beets have a graphical interface?
faqs.answer: It's primarily a command-line tool, but an optional web plugin provides browser-based library browsing and playback.

faqs.question: What does Beets use for metadata?
faqs.answer: Primarily the MusicBrainz database, with optional plugins for Discogs and Beatport.

faqs.question: Can Beets convert audio formats?
faqs.answer: Yes, transcoding is available through a plugin.

use_cases.title: Large library metadata cleanup
use_cases.description: Automatically identify and correct tags across a sprawling, inconsistently tagged music collection.

use_cases.title: Feeding a self-hosted music server
use_cases.description: Use Beets as the tagging and organization layer before serving music through Plex, Jellyfin, or Navidrome.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Beets is a free, open-source, plugin-based tool that organizes music libraries and corrects metadata using MusicBrainz.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source server-client platform for broadcasting audio over FM radio from Raspberry Pi devices.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: BotWave is an open-source platform for broadcasting audio files or live streams over FM radio using Raspberry Pi hardware. It supports both a standalone single-Pi setup and a server-client model where one central server manages and controls multiple Pi-based broadcast clients remotely.
The project is built by DPIP Studio, installs via a one-command automated script, and requires a Raspberry Pi with an antenna or wire connected to GPIO pin 4 (physical pin 7) to transmit.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: BotWave is aimed at hobbyists and makers experimenting with low-power FM broadcasting on Raspberry Pi, particularly those wanting to manage several Pi transmitters from one place. As with any FM transmission project, local radio spectrum regulations apply and should be checked before use.

features.title: Multi-device management
features.description: Control and monitor multiple Raspberry Pi broadcast clients from a single central server.

features.title: Audio broadcasting
features.description: Transmit local audio files or live streams over FM.

features.title: Remote file upload
features.description: Upload audio to a connected Pi client from anywhere the server is reachable.

features.title: Remote broadcast control
features.description: Start, stop, and manage broadcasts on clients remotely.

features.title: Passkey authentication
features.description: Secures client-server communication with passkey-based authentication.

features.title: Standalone mode
features.description: Run on a single Raspberry Pi without needing a separate server.

pros: Free and open source
One-command install script for quick setup
Supports managing multiple broadcast points from one server
Works standalone for simple single-device use
cons: Requires specific Raspberry Pi hardware and a GPIO antenna connection
FM broadcasting is subject to local radio regulations that vary by country
Newer, smaller project with limited third-party documentation

faqs.question: Is BotWave free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free and open source.

faqs.question: What hardware do I need?
faqs.answer: A Raspberry Pi with an antenna or wire connected to GPIO 4 (physical pin 7) for transmission.

faqs.question: Can I run BotWave without a server?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports a standalone mode on a single Raspberry Pi as well as a multi-client server-managed setup.

faqs.question: Is FM broadcasting with BotWave legal everywhere?
faqs.answer: Radio spectrum regulations vary by country, so users should confirm local rules on low-power FM transmission before broadcasting.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source radio broadcast automation platform for scheduling, library management, and AM/FM or web playout.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: LibreTime is a self-hosted radio broadcast and automation platform used to schedule, manage, and play out audio for community and internet radio stations. It began in 2017 as a community-driven fork of Sourcefabric's Airtime, created after Airtime's open-source development stalled in favor of a paid SaaS offering.

The platform combines a media library, a drag-and-drop scheduling calendar, and a playout engine that can drive traditional AM/FM transmitter chains as well as pure internet radio streams. Shows, sweepers, podcasts, and full-length programs can all be organized and queued for automatic broadcast.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source music server that streams local libraries and internet radio to Squeezebox and software players.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lyrion Music Server (LMS) is the community-maintained continuation of Logitech Media Server, the software originally built by Slim Devices to power Squeezebox network audio players. After Logitech ended official development and shut down its mysqueezebox.com cloud service in March 2024, the project was handed to the open-source community and rebranded as Lyrion Music Server starting with version 9.0 in November 2024.

Lyrion streams music from a local library, internet radio stations, and connected streaming services to a wide range of hardware and software players, including legacy Squeezebox devices and modern software players like Squeezelite and piCorePlayer. It can manage multiple synchronized players across different rooms or locations from a single server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Lyrion suits owners of legacy Squeezebox hardware who need continued server support, plus multi-room audio enthusiasts and self-hosters who want a locally controlled alternative to commercial streaming platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: moOde Audio is free, open-source software built exclusively for the Raspberry Pi family of single-board computers, turning inexpensive hardware into an audiophile-grade music streamer. It is developed by and for the DIY audio community, with the source code published on GitHub under GPLv3.

The player supports high-resolution and DSD audio, integrates renderers like Spotify Connect, AirPlay 2, and RoonBridge, and offers customizable audio processing through CamillaDSP with 64-bit volume control. It is controlled entirely through a browser-based interface, so no dedicated app is required on desktop, tablet, or phone.

enrichment.short_description: Extensible, open-source music server written in Python that plays local files, streaming services, and radio via extensions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mopidy is an extensible music server written in Python, first released in December 2009. Its core is deliberately minimal; nearly all of its capability — local file playback, Spotify, SoundCloud, internet radio, and more — is added through a large ecosystem of community-built extensions.

Because Mopidy exposes an MPD-compatible interface and its own HTTP/JSON API, it can be controlled by a wide range of existing MPD clients as well as dedicated Mopidy web and mobile frontends, making it a flexible backbone for headless music setups such as the Raspberry Pi-based Pi MusicBox distribution.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Mopidy appeals to developers and tinkerers who want a hackable, extension-driven music server, and to self-hosters running a headless music player (e.g., on a Raspberry Pi) that they control with a separate client app.

enrichment.short_description: Music Player Daemon is a flexible, server-side music player controlled remotely by third-party client applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Music Player Daemon (MPD) is a flexible, server-side application for playing music, first released in 2003. It runs as a background daemon on a server or single-board computer and exposes a network protocol that client applications connect to for playback control, rather than providing a built-in graphical interface itself.

MPD supports a wide range of audio formats through plugins and libraries, and it has spawned a large ecosystem of third-party clients across desktop, web, and mobile (including tools like myMPD and various iOS clients) that connect to a running MPD server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: MPD is aimed at technical users and self-hosters who want a lightweight, headless music playback backend that they can control with any of the many available third-party clients, rather than a single all-in-one app.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted tool that monitors your listening activity across many music sources and scrobbles it to Last.fm, ListenBrainz, and more.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: multi-scrobbler is a self-hosted, Docker-friendly application that monitors music listening activity across many sources and forwards ('scrobbles') it to one or more tracking services. It bridges the gap left by services that no longer natively scrobble everywhere you listen.

It supports a long list of sources — including Spotify, Plex, Jellyfin, YouTube Music, Kodi, Mopidy, VLC, and Subsonic-compatible servers like Navidrome and Airsonic — and can send scrobbles to destinations including Last.fm, ListenBrainz, Libre.fm, Maloja, Koito, and Discord. Failed scrobbles are queued and retried automatically, and a built-in web interface exposes stats, logs, and runtime configuration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: multi-scrobbler is built for self-hosters and music enthusiasts who listen across multiple apps and services and want a single, centralized, privacy-respecting scrobbling history — including those tracking listening for friends or family members.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source terminal-based music player, library manager, and streaming server for large local collections.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: musikcube is a fully functional, terminal-based music player, library manager, and streaming audio server that runs natively on Windows, macOS, and Linux, including on a Raspberry Pi with a custom DAC. It is built around three components: the core musikcube terminal player, the musikdroid Android companion app, and musikcore, a C++ library developers can build on.

It handles file scanning, tag indexing, gapless and crossfading playback, play queue management, and playlist CRUD operations, with an extensible plugin architecture and support for high-quality audio output up to 24-bit/192kHz. The project is designed to handle very large libraries, with reported support for collections exceeding 250,000 tracks.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: musikcube appeals to command-line-oriented music enthusiasts, home server hobbyists who want a lightweight streaming backend, and developers building on the musikcore library, including Raspberry Pi tinkerers assembling custom audio setups.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: PinePods is a self-hosted podcast management platform built in Rust that emphasizes performance and full data ownership. It provides a complete podcast ecosystem — playback position, settings, subscriptions, and themes all sync across every device you use.

Beyond standard podcast subscriptions, PinePods can subscribe to YouTube channels and deliver their audio as podcast-style downloads. It includes smart playlists with synchronized filtering, podcast discovery through the Podcast Index and iTunes search, multi-user support with separate libraries per family member, and rich episode metadata including chapters, transcripts, people information, and funding links. long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Swing Music is a self-hosted music streaming server designed to give it a modern, polished interface comparable to commercial streaming apps while keeping your library fully under your own control. It is open source under AGPLv3 and can be installed via a command-line script on Linux and Apple Silicon Macs, via Docker, or as a portable binary on Linux, Windows, and Mac.

Core features include folder-based browsing, typo-tolerant fuzzy search, automatic duplicate detection that favors higher-bitrate files, and collections for grouping albums and artists. Listening statistics are tracked for all users, while advanced discovery features such as personalized mixes are offered as a premium option supported through a one-time payment or GitHub sponsorship.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ClipBucket is an open-source PHP script that lets anyone launch their own video and photo sharing website. The current release, ClipBucket V5, is maintained by Oxygenz, which has developed the codebase since 2016 after the original ClipBucket project stalled and was later archived.

The self-hosted Community edition is free to download from GitHub; Oxygenz also offers an optional managed SaaS hosting plan and paid setup/migration services for people who don't want to run their own server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: ClipBucket suits organizations and communities that want a self-hosted, branded alternative to public video platforms — for internal video portals, niche video communities, or educational content libraries — and are comfortable running a PHP application on their own server.

features.title: Social Features

features.title: Multi-Server Infrastructure
features.description: Can be scaled across multiple servers for larger deployments.

Actively maintained (current version 5.5.0) after taking over from the original abandoned project
Rich social and video features out of the box (playlists, groups, messaging)
Supports modern formats: 4K, HLS conversion, Chromecast
cons: Requires you to provide and manage your own PHP hosting
The original ClipBucket project (pre-2016) was abandoned and archived in December 2022, which can cause confusion with the current Oxygenz-maintained V5
Managed hosting, priority support, and custom development require the paid SaaS plan

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source UPnP/DLNA media server that streams your personal media library to TVs, consoles, and mobile devices.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Gerbera is a free UPnP/DLNA media server that streams music, video, and photos to devices on a home network, including smart TVs, game consoles, and phones. It is a community-driven continuation of the MediaTomb project, built on the MediaTomb 0.12.1 codebase since 2016 and released under the GPLv2 license.

The server includes automatic metadata extraction, optional on-the-fly transcoding, and a JavaScript-based import layer for customizing how media is organized, all managed through a web-based configuration interface.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Gerbera is aimed at home users and self-hosters running Linux who want to serve a local media collection to standards-based DLNA/UPnP clients without relying on a proprietary media server.

features.description: Optionally converts media formats to match what the client device supports.

features.description: Customize how your library is organized using JavaScript-based import rules.

features.title: Web-Based Configuration UI
features.description: Manage the server through a browser-based interface.

features.description: Actively maintained fork that carries forward the well-known MediaTomb project.

Actively maintained, unlike the original MediaTomb project it forked from
Broad device compatibility through standard UPnP/DLNA
Scriptable metadata and import behavior for advanced customization
cons: UPnP/DLNA network setup can be technical for non-technical users
Advanced customization requires editing JavaScript import scripts
Designed for local network use, with no built-in remote/cloud access

faqs.answer: Yes, Gerbera continues development from the MediaTomb 0.12.1 codebase, starting in 2016.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source streaming media server for internet radio and audio/video broadcasting, maintained by Xiph.Org.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Icecast is free server software for streaming multimedia, first released in 1999 as an open alternative to proprietary streaming audio technologies of the time. It is maintained by the Xiph.Org Foundation, the same organization behind formats like Vorbis, Opus, FLAC, and Theora, and is distributed under the GNU GPL version 2.

Icecast 2 supports Ogg Vorbis, Ogg Theora, Opus, WebM, and MP3 streams, and can be used to run internet radio stations, jukeboxes, and other live audio or video streaming services. Recent versions add a web admin interface with dark mode, listener count tracking, TLS support, and X-Forwarded-For proxy handling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Icecast is built for broadcasters, hobbyist DJs, community radio operators, and developers who need a lightweight, standards-based server for distributing live audio or video streams over HTTP.

Lightweight and stable, with a long production track record
Supports a wide range of audio and video formats
Compatible with a large ecosystem of source client software (e.g., BUTT, ffmpeg)

Admin interface is functional but basic compared to commercial platforms
Requires a separate source client to broadcast to the server

faqs.answer: Running internet radio stations and streaming live audio or video.

faqs.answer: Yes, it is free software licensed under the GPLv2.

faqs.question: Who maintains Icecast?
faqs.answer: The Xiph.Org Foundation.

faqs.answer: Yes, formats such as WebM and Ogg Theora are supported alongside audio.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source self-hosted karaoke party system with a mobile-friendly song browser and queue.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Karaoke Eternal is a self-hosted, open-source karaoke system built for parties. It runs a server on your own hardware and lets guests find and queue songs directly from their phone's browser, with no app install required.

It supports MP3+G files and MP4 videos with music-synced visualizations, offers QR-code joining, and can run multiple simultaneous password-protected rooms with independent queues. The project states it includes no ads or telemetry.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source media center software for playing and organizing video, music, and photos across many platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kodi is a free and open-source home theater/media center application originally created in 2003 as XBMC and renamed Kodi in 2014. It is developed by the non-profit XBMC Foundation and a global community of volunteer contributors, and is released under the GPLv2 license.

Kodi plays and organizes local and network video, music, and photo files, and can be extended with skins and add-ons for streaming services, live TV/DVR backends, weather, and remote control. It's available for Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS, and tvOS.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Kodi suits home theater PC (HTPC) enthusiasts, cord-cutters, and anyone wanting a single, customizable hub for a personal video, music, and photo library across desktop, mobile, and TV devices.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted media server and proxy for routing RTSP, RTMP, HLS, WebRTC, and SRT streams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MediaMTX is a ready-to-use, open-source live media router that acts as both a server and a proxy. It handles publishing, reading, proxying, forwarding, recording, and playback of real-time video and audio streams across RTSP, RTMP, HLS, WebRTC, and SRT.
The project ships as a single self-contained binary with no external dependencies, is hosted on GitHub with a large community (20K+ stars), and is free to self-host with no licensing cost.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: MediaMTX is aimed at developers, security/surveillance integrators, and technical self-hosters who need to route live camera or streaming sources between different protocols without a graphical setup.

enrichment.short_description: Streaming media server toolkit with a free open-source edition and commercially licensed editions for OTT delivery.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MistServer is a full-featured streaming media toolkit for OTT (over-the-top internet streaming), originally developed by the Dutch company DDVTech B.V. (founded 2009). It supports a wide range of modern streaming protocols, including WebRTC, WHIP, SRT, RIST, and HLS.

MistServer offers a free, self-hostable open-source edition licensed under AGPLv3, alongside commercially licensed editions for organizations that need proprietary usage rights, additional features, or vendor support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: MistServer targets developers, system integrators, and media companies building OTT streaming infrastructure who need broad protocol support and the option to scale into a commercially supported deployment.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source UPnP/DLNA media server and renderer built for the GNOME desktop.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Rygel is a home media solution that implements both a UPnP AV MediaServer and MediaRenderer, letting you share audio, video, and pictures across a home network and control playback on other devices. It is part of the GNOME project, written largely in Vala, and released under the LGPL-2.0 license.

Through its plug-in architecture, Rygel can stream media from a PC to TVs, game consoles, and DLNA speakers, with optional on-the-fly transcoding for device compatibility. It also provides developer libraries (librygel-core, librygel-server, librygel-renderer) for building custom UPnP/DLNA applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Rygel is built for Linux and GNOME desktop users who want to share a local media library over UPnP/DLNA, and for developers building custom UPnP/DLNA server or renderer applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: üWave is an open-source, self-hosted platform for listening to media together as a group. Participants join a shared room and take turns adding songs, talks, or videos to a queue (the 'waitlist'), pulling content from source plugins for services like YouTube and SoundCloud.

The project is built as a Node.js/React stack, with the core server and web client published under the u-wave GitHub organization. There is no official managed SaaS version — running üWave means deploying your own server.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source streaming server that restreams live video from your own cameras or encoders to your site and platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Restreamer, built by datarhei, is a free, Apache-2.0-licensed Docker application for turning an H.264 video source — an IP camera, USB webcam, RaspiCam, or hardware encoder — into an HLS stream you can embed on your own website. It can simultaneously push the same feed to platforms like YouTube, Twitch, Facebook, or Vimeo, or to any generic RTMP server.
Because it runs entirely on your own Docker-capable infrastructure, there's no dependency on a third-party streaming provider or recurring subscription fee.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For

paragraphs: Anchr, created by developer Ferdinand Mütsch (GitHub: muety), is an open-source 'toolbox for tiny tasks on the internet' that combines a bookmark manager, an image hosting service, and a link shortener in one self-hosted app. Bookmarks can be organized into collections with descriptions to make them easier to search.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted proof-of-work reverse proxy that blocks AI scraper bots before they reach your site.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Anubis is an open-source, MIT-licensed reverse proxy created by developer Xe Iaso and now maintained by their company, Techaro. It sits in front of a website and requires each visitor's browser to solve a small SHA-256 proof-of-work challenge before serving a page. Legitimate human visitors barely notice the brief delay, while high-volume AI scrapers that ignore robots.txt face a steep, deliberately expensive computational cost for every page crawled.

The project was built after an AI crawler overwhelmed the author's own self-hosted Git server, and has since been adopted by projects including GNOME GitLab, the Linux kernel mailing list archive, FFmpeg, and Wine.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Open-source projects, self-hosted Git/forge instances, and any website operator being hit by aggressive AI/LLM training crawlers who want a free, self-hosted alternative to commercial bot-mitigation services.

features.description: Unrecognized visitors must solve a SHA-256 puzzle before being granted access to the site.

features.title: Known-bot allowlist
features.description: Configurable allowlist recognizes crawlers like Google, Bing, and DuckDuckGo via user-agent and reverse-DNS verification.

features.title: Persistent auth cookie
features.description: Verified visitors get a cookie so they aren't re-challenged for roughly a week.

features.description: Runs as a lightweight proxy in front of any existing origin server.

features.title: Open-source core
features.description: MIT-licensed and self-hostable via Docker or standalone binaries.

features.title: Optional reputation add-on
features.description: A paid service called Thoth adds GeoIP and BGP ASN-based reputation filtering on top of the free core.

Effective, low-friction deterrent against high-volume scraping
Simple reverse-proxy deployment in front of existing infrastructure
Widely adopted by well-known open-source infrastructure projects
Actively maintained
cons: Adds a JavaScript challenge step that can affect users with JS disabled or certain accessibility tools
Advanced GeoIP/reputation filtering requires the paid Thoth add-on
Requires running and maintaining an additional proxy component
May occasionally challenge legitimate bots not on the allowlist

faqs.question: Is Anubis free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Anubis proxy is open source under the MIT license and free to self-host; an optional paid add-on called Thoth adds GeoIP/ASN reputation filtering.

faqs.question: How does Anubis stop AI crawlers?
faqs.answer: It makes each visitor's browser solve a small proof-of-work puzzle, which is trivial for occasional human visitors but computationally expensive at the scale AI scrapers operate.

faqs.question: Does Anubis block search engines like Google?
faqs.answer: No, it includes a configurable allowlist for known-good crawlers such as Google, Bing, and DuckDuckGo, verified by user-agent and reverse DNS.

faqs.question: Who maintains Anubis?
faqs.answer: It was created by developer Xe Iaso and is maintained by their company, Techaro.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source terminal session recorder with a free hosted platform and a self-hostable server for saving recordings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: asciinema is a free and open-source tool for recording terminal sessions as lightweight, text-based recordings — rather than video — and sharing them on the web. The command-line recorder captures a session with a single `asciinema rec` command, and recordings can be played back, embedded in blogs or docs, or streamed live.

The companion asciinema-server, built with Elixir and Phoenix, powers the free public asciinema.org platform and can also be self-hosted, giving teams full control over where their recordings are stored.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For

paragraphs: CUPS (Common Unix Printing System) is the standards-based, open-source printing system developed by Apple for macOS, iOS, and iPadOS, and made available for other Unix-like operating systems through the OpenPrinting project. enrichment.short_description: Domain portfolio manager that tracks expiration, DNS, SSL, and security across every registrar in one dashboard.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Domain Locker is a domain portfolio management platform that centralizes monitoring for domains spread across multiple registrars. It tracks expiration dates, DNS records, SSL certificates, WHOIS changes, and uptime, and can alert owners before a domain lapses or its configuration changes unexpectedly.

The project is open source under the MIT license, so it can be self-hosted for free on your own infrastructure. A hosted cloud version is also offered for people who would rather not run their own server, with paid tiers based on how many domains you manage.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Domain investors, agencies managing client portfolios, and sysadmins who want a single place to watch expiration dates, SSL status, and DNS changes across many domains rather than checking each registrar separately.

features.title: Automatic asset detection

features.title: Expiration and change alerts
features.description: Notifies you before a domain expires or when its configuration changes, via email, webhook, Telegram, or Signal.

features.title: Security audits
features.description: Flags security issues and gives recommendations for each domain in your portfolio.

features.title: Uptime and performance monitoring
features.description: Tracks whether domains are online and responding as expected.

features.title: Financial tracking
features.description: Records registration costs, renewal fees, and estimated valuations for profit/loss tracking.

features.title: API and feed access
features.description: Exposes data via a REST API, Prometheus metrics, iCal, and RSS for integration with other tools.

pros: Open source and free to self-host on your own server
Multi-channel alerting beyond just email
Tracks financial value of a domain portfolio, not just DNS/SSL status
API access for integrating with existing monitoring stacks
Discounted pricing for nonprofits and open-source maintainers
cons: Free cloud tier is capped at 5 domains
Larger portfolios require a paid cloud plan or self-hosting effort
Self-hosting requires setting up and maintaining your own server

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source contest judging system used to run ICPC-style programming competitions with automated grading.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: DOMjudge is a free, open-source system for running programming contests, most notably ICPC-style competitions. It automates the judging of submitted code against test cases, provides a jury interface for manual review, and runs a live scoreboard for teams and spectators.

The project has been used in real competitions since 2004, including numerous ICPC regional contests such as NWERC and SWERC, and is also used in university courses and online judging systems. It's released under the GNU General Public License and maintained by a community of contributors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Contest organizers, universities running programming competitions, and instructors who need automated code judging for classroom exercises or coding contests.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted platform for running experience-sampling and ecological momentary assessment research studies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ESMira is an open-source, decentralized platform for running Experience Sampling Method (ESM), Ambulatory Assessment (AA), and Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) research studies. It's developed by the Department of Psychological Methodology at Karl Landsteiner Privatuniversität in Austria and described in a peer-reviewed paper published in Behavior Research Methods.

Researchers install ESMira on their own PHP-enabled webserver, where they design questionnaires, schedule prompts sent to participants' phones, and collect fully anonymous data without routing it through any third-party server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Academic researchers and study administrators running ESM, EMA, or ambulatory diary-style studies in psychology, medicine, or behavioral science who need participant data to stay under their own control.

features.title: Decentralized self-hosting
features.description: Each study administrator hosts their own instance, so studies and their data operate independently on separate servers.

features.title: Participant anonymity
features.description: Designed so participant data is never sent anywhere beyond the server hosting that specific study.

features.title: PHP-only server requirement
features.description: Runs on any standard webserver with PHP, without complex infrastructure dependencies.

features.title: Automatic self-updates
features.description: Can back itself up, pull the newest version from GitHub, and migrate its data structure automatically.

features.title: Customizable studies
features.description: Lets administrators configure surveys, prompt schedules, and data collection rules for their specific research design.

pros: Participant data never leaves the researcher's own server
Free and open source under AGPL
Simple PHP-only hosting requirement, no exotic infrastructure
Backed by an academic department with a published methodology paper
Built-in self-update mechanism
cons: Requires the researcher to set up and maintain their own server
Documentation and design are oriented toward academic researchers, not general users
Niche, research-specific tool rather than a general-purpose survey platform
Interface is less polished than commercial EMA/EMS tools

faqs.question: What is ESMira used for?
faqs.answer: It's used by researchers to run experience-sampling, ambulatory assessment, and ecological momentary assessment studies, sending scheduled prompts to participants and collecting their responses.

faqs.question: Is ESMira free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the AGPL license.

faqs.question: Do I need to be a developer to set it up?
faqs.answer: You need access to a PHP-enabled webserver, but the project is designed so study administrators without deep technical expertise can install and configure it.

faqs.question: How is participant privacy protected?
faqs.answer: Because each study runs on its own decentralized server, participant data is only ever sent to the server hosting that specific study, not to any central third party.

enrichment.short_description: Community-run, free and open-source app catalog offering FOSS Android apps outside the Google Play Store.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: F-Droid is an installable catalog of free and open source software (FOSS) applications for Android, functioning as an alternative to the Google Play Store. It has operated since 2010, maintained by community contributors and funded through donations rather than corporate ownership.

Because F-Droid builds many of the apps it distributes itself from public source code, it emphasizes verifiability and freedom from tracking, and organizations can also run their own private F-Droid repository.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Privacy-conscious Android users who want FOSS alternatives to mainstream apps, open-source developers distributing apps outside Google Play, and organizations that want to self-host an internal app repository.

Anyone can self-host a custom app repository

Android only

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source Docker app that resizes, crops, and compresses images on the fly, like a self-hosted Cloudinary.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Flyimg is a Dockerized, open-source image-processing service that resizes, crops, and optimizes images on the fly using ImageMagick, offering a self-hosted alternative to hosted image CDNs like Cloudinary. It fetches a source image, applies the requested transformations via URL parameters, caches the output, and serves optimized files.

The project is released under the AGPL-3.0 license, is available as a single Docker container, and also offers an optional hosted SaaS version at flyimg.io for teams that don't want to run their own infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Developers and teams who want an on-premise, self-hosted alternative to commercial image CDNs for resizing, cropping, and format-optimizing images at request time.

features.description: The o_auto option selects the best output format based on the browser's Accept header.

features.description: Stores processed images so repeat requests are served from cache instead of reprocessed.

features.description: Supports face-aware cropping and blurring as part of the image pipeline.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted app for cataloging, tagging, and sharing personal collections such as hobby items, cards, or memorabilia.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Koillection is a free, open-source, self-hosted platform for cataloging and organizing personal collections. Users can create collections and items, attach photos and details, and organize everything with flexible tags and custom 'signs' rather than rigid categories.

Because it runs on your own server, all uploaded data and images stay under your control. Koillection also supports wishlists, a REST API for custom integrations, and works as a Progressive Web App so it can be used comfortably from a phone or tablet browser.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Koillection suits hobbyist collectors — of trading cards, stamps, figurines, books, or any other physical items — who want a structured digital catalog without handing their data to a third-party service. It also fits anyone who wants to selectively share parts of a collection with others while keeping full control over what is visible.

enrichment.short_description: AI-based grammar, spelling, and style checker with browser extensions, desktop apps, and an open-source self-hostable core.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: LanguageTool is a writing assistant that checks text for grammar, spelling, punctuation, and style issues across more than 20 languages. It began as an open-source grammar-checking project and is now also offered as a commercial product with browser extensions, desktop apps, and office integrations.

Beyond basic spellchecking, LanguageTool flags awkward phrasing, redundant words, and complex punctuation problems, and its Premium tier adds a paraphrasing tool that rewrites sentences to be more formal, concise, or fluent. The underlying open-source engine can also be self-hosted, letting developers run grammar checking on their own infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: LanguageTool is aimed at writers, students, non-native speakers, and businesses that need multilingual grammar and style checking across documents, email, and web forms. Developers who want a self-hosted, privacy-preserving grammar-checking API can run the open-source core directly.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source machine translation API that can run fully offline and self-hosted, or via a paid hosted API.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: LibreTranslate is a free and open-source machine translation API, powered by the Argos Translate engine, that can be self-hosted and run entirely offline once its language models are downloaded. It supports translating text and files across a range of languages via a simple JSON API.

For teams that don't want to manage their own infrastructure, a hosted API is available through the LibreTranslate portal on Pro and Business plans, alongside a free, rate-limited public endpoint that doesn't require an API key.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: LibreTranslate fits developers and organizations that need translation capability without sending text to third-party cloud services — for example, apps handling sensitive documents, air-gapped environments, or products that want to avoid recurring API costs by self-hosting.

enrichment.short_description: Free, self-hosted spam protection for web forms that filters submissions by content instead of using CAPTCHA puzzles.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: mosparo is a self-hosted spam protection system for web forms. Rather than asking users to solve a CAPTCHA challenge, it checks the actual content submitted through a form — comparable to how an email spam filter works — using only the entered data plus the IP address and user agent, which are automatically hashed or encrypted.

The widget's design can be customized to match the look of the site it's embedded in, and submitted form data is automatically deleted after 14 days. mosparo is free, open source, and designed to be accessible to screen-reader users and anyone using a mouse, keyboard, or touch input.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: mosparo fits website owners and developers who want to block spam on contact, signup, or comment forms without burdening legitimate visitors with CAPTCHA puzzles, and who prefer to self-host their spam-filtering infrastructure rather than depend on a third-party service.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted virtual browser in Docker that streams a shared, remote browsing session over WebRTC with low latency.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Neko is a self-hosted virtual browser that runs inside a Docker container and streams its interface to participants over WebRTC, with latency of roughly 300ms or less. Multiple people can join the same session and take turns controlling the browser, making it useful for real-time collaboration.

Sessions can either persist across devices or be run as throwaway, isolated instances with no history, cookies, or cache retained locally. Because only video and audio are streamed to participants, the underlying browsing activity and network access stay on the server, which also lets Neko act as a jump host into internal resources or as a live broadcast source for platforms like Twitch and YouTube.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Neko fits developers pairing on debugging sessions, educators or streamers who want to broadcast a shared browser, teams needing a remote jump-host browser for internal resources, and privacy-conscious users who want disposable, local-trace-free browsing sessions.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source weather API offering free non-commercial forecasts and historical data from 15+ national weather services.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (up to 10,000 non-commercial calls/day); commercial plans from $29/month

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Open-Meteo is an open-source weather API that combines more than 30 weather models from over 15 national meteorological services — including ECMWF, NOAA, DWD, and Météo-France — into a single, simple HTTP GET interface that requires no authentication for non-commercial use.

It also provides deep historical weather data via ERA5 reanalysis dating back to January 1940, with output available in JSON, CSV, or XLSX formats. The codebase is open source under AGPLv3, so organizations can self-host their own instance for unlimited calls, air-gapped environments, or high-volume machine learning workloads.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Open-Meteo fits developers who want weather data in a hobby or non-commercial project without API-key setup, plus researchers, climate scientists, and machine learning engineers who need a long historical weather archive. Commercial weather applications can use paid plans with dedicated endpoints and a commercial license.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hostable text-to-speech reader that turns EPUB, PDF, DOCX, and other documents into synced read-along audio.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenReader is an open-source document reader that turns EPUB, PDF, DOCX, TXT, and Markdown files into a synchronized read-along experience with multilingual text-to-speech and word-level highlighting. It uses layout-aware PDF parsing to preserve document structure and can export finished audio as an audiobook.

It supports multiple text-to-speech providers, including self-hosted, OpenAI-compatible TTS servers such as Kokoro-FastAPI and Orpheus-FastAPI, as well as cloud APIs like OpenAI, Replicate, and DeepInfra — so users can choose between a fully local, private setup or a cloud-based one.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: OpenReader is well suited to people who benefit from read-along audio, such as those with dyslexia or visual impairments, and to self-hosters who want a private alternative to commercial text-to-speech reading apps for converting ebooks and PDFs into audio.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source zero trust networking platform that secures services by identity instead of IP address, sponsored by NetFoundry.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenZiti is an open-source zero trust networking platform that replaces traditional IP-based network security with identity-based access control, encrypting all traffic end-to-end with libsodium-based cryptography. Services protected by OpenZiti can become effectively invisible on the network, resolved only through an authenticated private DNS rather than a public IP address.

The project offers flexible deployment models: embedding zero trust directly into applications via SDKs for the strongest security, using tunnelers for host-level zero trust access in existing environments, or covering network-level access where apps and hosts can't be modified. OpenZiti is sponsored by NetFoundry, which also offers managed and commercially supported self-hosted options built on the same open-source project.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: OpenZiti fits engineering and security teams looking to replace or augment traditional VPNs with a true zero trust model, and developers who want to embed zero trust access directly into their applications through an SDK rather than relying solely on network-level tunneling.

features.description: Protected services are not discoverable through normal network scans.

features.description: Uses libsodium-based cryptography to secure all traffic in transit.

features.title: Embedded SDKs
features.description: Lets developers build zero trust access directly into applications (ZTAA).

features.title: Tunneler-Based Access
features.description: Provides zero trust host access (ZTHA) for existing apps without code changes.

Fully open source with an active GitHub community

Multiple deployment models fit different levels of application integration
Backed by NetFoundry with optional commercial support and managed hosting

Production deployments often benefit from NetFoundry's paid support or managed tiers
Full protection requires embedding SDKs or deploying tunnelers across your environment
Self-hosting the full control plane requires networking and security expertise

faqs.answer: OpenZiti is an open-source project sponsored by NetFoundry.

faqs.answer: ZTAA embeds zero trust directly into applications via SDK for the strongest security, ZTHA uses tunnelers for host-level access in existing production environments, and ZTNA covers network-level access when apps or hosts can't be modified.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: reveal.js is an open-source HTML presentation framework created by Hakim El Hattab in 2011 and still actively maintained today. It turns standard HTML, CSS, and JavaScript into fully-featured slide decks that run in any web browser, with support for nested/vertical slides, Markdown-authored content, code syntax highlighting, LaTeX/math rendering, and PDF export.

Because presentations are just web pages, they can embed live web content via iframes, be styled with custom CSS, and be extended through a JavaScript API and plugin system. The core framework is free; its creator also offers Slides.com, an optional paid visual editor built on top of reveal.js for people who prefer a GUI over hand-written HTML/Markdown.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source SANE (Scanner Access Now Easy) API and daemon that let you share and access scanners over a network.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: SANE (Scanner Access Now Easy) is an open-source API and set of backend drivers, originally released in 1996, that provides standardized access to image scanners and similar capture devices, primarily on Unix and Linux systems. The SANE API itself is public domain, while the reference implementation is distributed under the GNU GPL.

The 'network scanning' capability referenced here comes from SANE's saned daemon and net backend: saned runs on the machine physically connected to a scanner and exposes it to permitted hosts on the network, while client machines use the net backend to access that remote scanner as if it were local. This lets a single scanner be shared across multiple computers on a network without dedicated network-scanner hardware.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: SANE is aimed at Linux/Unix users, self-hosters, and system administrators who want to share a scanner across a home or office network, or who need a standardized scanning API for building scanning frontends and applications.

features.title: net backend
features.description: Lets client machines connect to a remote scanner shared via saned as though it were a local device.

features.description: Backend drivers exist for a wide range of flatbed, handheld, and other scanner hardware.

features.title: Standard protocol support
features.description: Includes backends for modern network scanning protocols such as AirScan/eSCL and WSD.

features.description: A command-line frontend for scripting and automating scans.

No official web-based UI — it's a system-level API/daemon, not an end-user app

faqs.question: How does SANE support network scanning?
faqs.answer: The saned daemon runs on the host physically connected to the scanner and shares it with permitted network hosts; those clients use SANE's net backend to access it remotely.

faqs.answer: Yes. The SANE API is public domain, and the reference implementation is released under the GNU GPL.
